beats.narration_text: Ciao — this is Liam, in for Bear. Ask Claude to build a pitch deck, parse a presentation, or update existing slides. The PPTX skill fires. It does not reach for python-pptx. It routes: markitdown to read, the editing workflow for template work, pptxgenjs to create from scratch. And it has a design mandate — boring slides are explicitly prohibited. Let me show you the routing logic, the design rules, and the QA standard that makes this skill serious.

props.command: Create a 10-slide pitch deck for our SaaS product. Bold design, no boring templates. Use our brand colors: teal and gold.
output: Path: create from scratch → reading pptxgenjs.md
Design: commit to one visual motif, dark title + light content sandwich

beats.narration_text: The skill routes across three paths. If the task is to read or analyze content, run markitdown against the pptx file — it extracts all text cleanly. If the task is to edit or modify an existing presentation, read editing.md: analyze the template with thumbnail dot py, unpack the slide XML, make changes, then repack. If the task is to create from scratch with no template, read pptxgenjs.md and build with pptxgenjs. After any generation, QA is mandatory in two stages: content QA with markitdown and a grep for placeholder text, then visual QA — convert slides to images and inspect them with subagents who have fresh eyes. The skill is explicit: assume there are problems. Your first render is almost never correct.

props.sparkLine: Read. Edit template. Create scratch. Then QA.

beats.act: self-demo — design rules verbatim from SKILL.md
beats.narration_text: The design section is the most distinctive part of the skill. Before picking any colors, the rule is: the palette should feel designed for this specific topic — if swapping your colors into a different presentation would still work, you haven't made specific enough choices. One color should dominate sixty to seventy percent of the visual weight, with one or two supporting tones and a sharp accent. Commit to one visual motif and carry it across every slide. Dark slides for title and conclusion, light for content — the sandwich structure. For typography: never default to Arial. Pick a header font with personality. For QA, the rule is explicit: use subagents for visual inspection. You've been staring at the code and will see what you expect, not what's there. And the hard rule: never use accent lines under titles — that is a hallmark of AI-generated slides.

props.sparkLine: Design for this topic. Not for any topic.

beats.act: teardown — QA-first insight + what it gets right/bites
beats.narration_text: Here is the teardown. The architectural insight of the PPTX skill is that it treats design and QA as first-class requirements, not afterthoughts. The skill has a routing table for three distinct paths, an explicit design constitution with named palettes and typography rules, and a QA mandate that says assume there are problems and use subagents with fresh eyes. What it gets right: the subagent QA requirement prevents the common failure mode of approving slides that look fine in code but are broken visually. The design rules give concrete, specific guidance instead of vague advice. Where it bites: pptxgenjs requires npm and creates JavaScript-based slides that may need extra post-processing. The editing.md workflow involves XML manipulation which is error-prone. And the design rules are prescriptive enough that they can conflict with a client's existing brand guidelines.

props.sparkLine: Design and QA are requirements. Not afterthoughts.

beats.narration_text: Verdict. The PPTX skill routes across three paths: markitdown to read, editing.md workflow to edit a template, pptxgenjs to create from scratch. Design is mandatory: one dominant color at 60-70%, a committed visual motif, dark-light sandwich structure, never accent lines under titles. QA is two stages: content QA with markitdown and placeholder grep, then visual QA with subagents who have fresh eyes. The rule: assume there are problems. Your first render is almost never correct.

artifactLines: 3 paths: markitdown (read) · editing.md (edit template) · pptxgenjs (create scratch)
Design: one dominant color (60-70%), one visual motif, dark/light sandwich
Typography: never default to Arial; pair header font with personality + clean body
Never use accent lines under titles — hallmark of AI-generated slides
QA stage 1: markitdown + grep for placeholders (xxxx, lorem, ipsum)
QA stage 2: subagents with fresh eyes — assume there are problems

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Open a Claude session. Paste this: Create a 5-slide investor pitch deck for a climate tech startup. The company makes carbon-capture hardware. Bold design — pick a palette that feels specific to this topic, commit to one visual motif, use the dark-light sandwich structure. Use the PPTX skill. Then watch two things: first, does Claude read pptxgenjs.md before starting? Second, after generating, does Claude run the QA sequence — content check with markitdown, then visual inspection with a subagent? If Claude generates slides and declares success without a visual QA pass, the QA mandate was ignored. And check the slides themselves: is there an accent line under any title? If yes, the design rules were not followed. That is the gate.

props.command: 5-slide investor pitch for a climate tech startup (carbon-capture hardware). Bold design, topic-specific palette, dark-light sandwich, one visual motif. Use the PPTX skill.
props.runningText: Watch: does Claude read pptxgenjs.md first? Does it run visual QA with subagents after?
output: Expected: reads pptxgenjs.md before writing any code
Expected: visual QA pass with subagent after generation
Red flag: no QA pass, or accent lines under slide titles
